titel:
Charles XII

(Please provide the text you would like me to translate from Swedish into English.)
html:
Charles XII, born on June 17 (June 27 according to the old calendar) in 1682 at Tre Kronor Castle in Stockholm, and died on November 30 (December 11 according to the old calendar) in 1718 in Fredrikshald, Norway, was King of Sweden from 1697 until his death. He was also Duke of Palatinate-Zweibrücken between 1697 and 1718, under the name Charles II.

In 1697, Charles XII inherited the power of the Swedish empire after the death of his father, Charles XI. He spent most of his reign on campaigns during the Great Northern War, where he fought against a triple alliance consisting of Denmark-Norway, Saxony-Poland, and Russia from 1700 onwards. The king's leadership and tactical skills contributed to initial successes in the war. At the beginning of the war, he forced Denmark-Norway into peace and defeated the Russian army at the Battle of Narva. Later, Charles XII invaded both Poland and Saxony, and after a prolonged campaign, he defeated his cousin August (II) the Strong's Saxon army and deposed him as King of Poland.

The tide turned against Charles XII during the campaign against Russia, which he began in the autumn of 1707 and ended with his decisive defeat at the Battle of Poltava in 1709. The king's army surrendered to the Russians, and Sweden's former enemies were able to return to the war. These later seized control of several Swedish territories. Charles XII fled to the Ottoman Empire, where he stayed for several years and from which he ruled the Swedish kingdom. He failed to coordinate a joint campaign against his enemies through an alliance with the Ottomans; they gave him the Turkish nickname "Demirbaş" ("Iron Head"). Charles XII returned to Sweden in 1715 to fight Denmark-Norway. He implemented several tax reforms to finance his continued warfare, which resulted in an economic crisis in Sweden and a heavy burden on Sweden's war- and plague-stricken population. His first attack on Norway in 1716 failed due to food shortages. A renewed and larger attack on Norway in 1718 was abruptly ended when he was shot dead during the siege of Fredrikstens fortress.

Charles XII's death and the consequences of the Great Northern War resulted in extensive territorial losses and immense human suffering for the defeated Swedish kingdom, ultimately leading to the end of Sweden's era as a great power. Since the king was unmarried and had no children, his sister Ulrika Eleonora became the reigning Queen of Sweden, although she soon abdicated in favor of her husband, Frederick I. In reaction to Charles XII's autocratic rule, the Carolingian absolute monarchy was abolished, and power shifted to the Riksdag (Swedish Parliament), marking the beginning of a period known as the Age of Liberty.

Charles XII's reign is one of the most discussed and controversial periods in Swedish history. His life has inspired numerous writings and cultural works, including a popular biography written by the philosopher Voltaire. His legacy and the circumstances surrounding his death have sometimes been the subject of polarized debate.

== Title ==

Charles XII's full title in Swedish was:

"We, Charles, by the Grace of God, King of Sweden, Goths and Vandals, Grand Duke of Finland, Duke in Scania, Estonia, Livonia, Karelia, Bremen, Verden, Stettin, Pomerania, Kashubia and Venden, Prince of Rügen, Lord of Ingria and Wismar, and also Palatinate Count on the Rhine, in Bavaria, and Duke of Jülich, Cleves and Berg."

== Childhood and Upbringing (1682–1697) == Charles XII was born at Tre Kronor Castle in Stockholm on Saturday, June 17th according to the old style calendar (June 27th, new style), 1682, as the son of King Charles XI and Queen Ulrika Eleonora the Elder. He was the second of the royal couple's seven children, of whom only Charles and his sisters Hedvig Sofia and Ulrika Eleonora survived to adulthood. The king wrote in his diary:

"Charles was born with a 'victory cap,' meaning a piece of the amniotic sac remained on his head." On July 12th, 1682, the young prince was baptized at the castle by the officiating minister Samuel Werenius. His godfathers were Prince George of Denmark, Duke Christian Albrecht of Holstein-Gottorp, and the Prince-Bishop of Lübeck. The former Queen Christina served as a godmother, along with Maria Amalia of Courland and Princess Sophia Dorothea of Holstein.

Ulrika Eleonora played a significant role in her children's upbringing. The young prince Charles learned morning and evening prayers, as well as biblical stories, from his mother. The queen organized public dinners to accustom the children to attention and took them with her to military exercises at Djurgården. As the eldest son in the royal family, Charles was the Crown Prince. Charles XI ordered that he receive a comprehensive education to prepare him to one day assume power over Sweden as an absolute monarch, a system of absolutism that Charles XI himself introduced to the kingdom through the Riksdags (parliaments) of 1680 and 1682, based on the theory of the divine right of kings.

=== Education ===

At the age of four, Charles was assigned his first tutor, Andreas Nordenhielm, who taught him reading, writing, languages, history, and moral principles for eight years. At six

How certain can we be about this?

Figures from the era of great powers are extensively documented in archives and contemporary publications. Much of what is known about who they were and what they did is well-established; the uncertainty lies in interpretation and motivation.
tldr: Charles XII was a Swedish king who ruled the country during a period when Sweden was a major power. His lengthy wars resulted in significant losses for Sweden and altered how the country was governed after his death.
lattlast:
In 1697, Karl XII became King of Sweden after the death of his father. He spent most of his reign at war against countries like Denmark, Russia, and Poland. Initially, he won many battles, but after a major defeat against the Russians in 1709, the situation became much more difficult for Sweden.

During the war, the king introduced new rules for collecting funds to continue fighting. This led to an economic crisis, making life very hard for the people of Sweden who suffered from both poverty and disease. Karl XII died in 1718 while attempting to attack Norway.

Because the king died without any children, his sister became queen, but she soon relinquished power to her husband. After his death, the rules governing Sweden's government were changed so that power was no longer held by a single person who made all the decisions, but was shared with the parliament (Riksdagen). This marked the end of Sweden's era as a major European power.
